summary: McKenzie recommends Firebug, a Firefox addon that lets you inspect and modify CSS properties in real time without page refreshes. He used it to fix long-standing visual bugs on Bingo Card Creator's site that he'd been putting off for over a year, including a 12-pixel alignment issue and undersized header fonts. The post illustrates his broader philosophy of incremental improvement and his self-awareness about lacking design skills.
key_points: Firebug enables real-time CSS inspection and modification, eliminating the edit-refresh cycle that discourages non-designers from fixing visual issues
Small visual bugs can persist for over a year when the barrier to fixing them is high enough, even when customers report them
Having the right tool gave McKenzie a sense of ownership over his design, lowering the psychological barrier to making changes
Progress in a small software business often comes through incremental improvements rather than dramatic overhauls
money_quotes: It can help answer the big (and befuddling) questions like What Line In My 2,000 Line CSS or HTML File Makes This Box Go Here, which throw non-designer me for a loop.
I always thanked them for the comment and made a mental note to do something about it, then (when I actually opened the CSS file) got lost and gave up.

It also gives me a feeling of ownership of the design, which (since I am not a designer and have the artistic acuity of a nearsighted mole) I generally treat like holy writ that I am not fit to alter.
11 pixels for a page header… not a quality choice! But I put up with it for forever.
It isn't a quantum leap in quality, obviously — just an evolution of inches, like most of the things I do.
key_insight: The right tooling can eliminate psychological barriers that cause small but meaningful improvements to be deferred indefinitely.
